qse_best_practices.overview: Guidelines and best practices for using the Quantum Safe Explorer mode effectively, ensuring accurate scans, proper command construction, and clear communication with users.

principle.name: Always Detect Before Executing
principle.description: Never assume the project language. Always use list_files to detect the language before constructing QSE commands.
principle.rationale: Different languages require different scan types and parameters. Incorrect language detection leads to failed scans or incomplete analysis.

example.good: 1. Use list_files to examine project structure 2. Count file extensions and check for language markers 3. Determine language with confidence 4. Construct appropriate command
example.bad: Assume Java because user mentioned "class files" without verification

principle.name: Java Requires Both Scans
principle.description: For complete quantum readiness assessment of Java projects, ALWAYS run both Cryptographic Analytics (with -da) and API Discovery (without -da) scans.
principle.rationale: Cryptographic Analytics analyzes bytecode for deep cryptographic usage patterns, while API Discovery examines source code for API calls. Both are necessary for comprehensive analysis.

principle.name: Use Absolute Paths
principle.description: Prefer absolute paths over relative paths when constructing QSE commands.
principle.rationale: The command changes directory to /usr/local/bin/qse-cli-artifacts before executing, so relative paths may not resolve correctly.

principle.name: Verify Paths Before Execution
principle.description: Always verify that input paths exist before constructing and executing commands.
principle.rationale: Prevents command failures and provides better error messages to users.
principle.implementation: Use list_files tool to verify directory exists and contains expected files

principle.name: Don't Parse QSE Output
principle.description: Do not attempt to parse, interpret, or summarize QSE scan results.
principle.rationale: QSE generates comprehensive reports in its own format. Your role is to execute the scans correctly, not to interpret the results.
principle.correct_behavior: Simply confirm that the scan completed successfully and inform the user that results are available in QSE output.

practice.name: Count Files, Not Just Check Existence
practice.description: Don't just check if .java files exist - count how many of each type exist to determine the dominant language.
practice.example: Project with 100 .java files and 5 .py files → Java project Project with 10 .java files and 50 .go files → Go project

practice.name: Check Language-Specific Markers
practice.description: Use project configuration files to confirm language detection.

practice.name: Exclude Build and Dependency Directories
practice.description: When counting files, exclude common build output and dependency directories.
practice.exclude_patterns: node_modules/, vendor/, .git/, build/, dist/, target/, __pycache__/, .venv/, venv/, .idea/, .vscode/

practice.name: Handle Ambiguity Gracefully
practice.description: When language detection is uncertain (multiple languages with similar counts), ask the user to specify rather than guessing.
practice.threshold: If two languages are within 20% of each other in file count, consider it ambiguous

practice.name: Follow Exact Command Structure
practice.description: Always use the exact command structure: cd to QSE directory, then execute cli.sh

practice.name: Parameter Order Consistency
practice.description: Maintain consistent parameter order for readability and debugging.

practice.name: Java Class File Path Logic
practice.description: For Java projects, search for class files in standard locations.
practice.implementation: Use list_files to check each location in order, use first found

practice.name: Flag Usage Rules
practice.description: Strict rules for when to include -da and -cf flags.

rules.rule: -da flag: ONLY for Java Cryptographic Analytics scan
-cf flag: ONLY for Java Cryptographic Analytics scan
API Discovery: NEVER include -da or -cf flags
Non-Java languages: NEVER include -da or -cf flags

practice.name: Execute Commands Sequentially
practice.description: For Java projects requiring both scans, execute them one at a time, waiting for each to complete before starting the next.

practice.name: Use execute_command Tool Correctly
practice.description: Always use the execute_command tool with the complete command string.

practice.name: Monitor for Errors
practice.description: Check command execution results for errors and handle them appropriately.

common_errors.error: Path not found → Verify input path exists
Command not found → Verify QSE installation path

practice.name: Be Clear About Scan Types
practice.description: Clearly communicate which scan type(s) are being executed and why.

examples.for_java: "Running complete quantum readiness assessment for Java project: 1. Cryptographic Analytics scan (analyzing bytecode) 2. API Discovery scan (analyzing source code)"
examples.for_other: "Running API Discovery scan for [language] project to identify quantum-vulnerable cryptographic APIs."

practice.name: Confirm Completion Simply
practice.description: After scan completion, provide simple confirmation without attempting to interpret results.
practice.template: "[Scan type] completed successfully for [language] project. Results are available in the QSE output directory."

practice.name: Ask Clarifying Questions When Needed
practice.description: When language detection fails or is ambiguous, ask the user to specify.
practice.use_ask_followup_question: Provide clear options with explanations of what each language supports

practice.name: Explain Limitations Clearly
practice.description: If user requests Cryptographic Analytics for non-Java language, explain the limitation clearly.
practice.example: "Cryptographic Analytics is only available for Java projects. For [language] projects, I can run API Discovery scan which identifies quantum-vulnerable cryptographic API usage."

practice.name: Graceful Degradation
practice.description: When class files aren't found for Java, use input path as fallback rather than failing.
practice.rationale: QSE can still perform useful analysis even without compiled class files

practice.name: Provide Actionable Error Messages
practice.description: When errors occur, explain what went wrong and what the user can do.

example.good: "Scan failed because the specified directory doesn't exist. Please verify the path: /path/to/project"

practice.name: Validate Before Executing
practice.description: Catch potential errors before executing commands.

validations.check: For Java: at least attempt to find class files

practice.name: Minimize Tool Calls
practice.description: Use list_files efficiently - get all needed information in one call when possible.

example.efficient: One list_files call with recursive=true to get full project structure, then analyze locally to detect language and find class files
example.inefficient: Multiple list_files calls for each directory separately

practice.name: Cache Detection Results
practice.description: Once language is detected, don't re-detect for subsequent scans in the same session.

practice.name: Batch Java Scans Mentally
practice.description: When planning Java scans, prepare both commands before execution to ensure consistency.

category.name: before_execution
category.item: Language detected or specified by user
For Java: class file path determined

category.name: after_execution
category.item: No attempt to parse QSE output

pitfall.mistake: Running only API Discovery for Java projects
pitfall.why_wrong: Misses deep bytecode analysis from Cryptographic Analytics
pitfall.correct: Always run both scans for Java quantum readiness

pitfall.mistake: Including -da flag for API Discovery scan
pitfall.why_wrong: -da is only for Cryptographic Analytics mode
pitfall.correct: Only include -da for Cryptographic Analytics scan
Only use -cf for Java Cryptographic Analytics

pitfall.why_wrong: Leads to wrong scan configuration and failures
pitfall.correct: Always detect language using list_files first

pitfall.why_wrong: May not resolve correctly after cd to QSE directory
pitfall.correct: Use absolute paths for clarity and reliability

pitfall.why_wrong: QSE generates its own comprehensive reports
pitfall.correct: Simply confirm scan completion, don't parse results

guideline.name: Stay Focused on Scanning
guideline.description: This mode's purpose is to execute QSE scans, not to provide general quantum computing advice or cryptography recommendations.

scope.in_scope: Detecting language, constructing commands, executing scans

guideline.name: Respect Tool Boundaries
guideline.description: This mode has read and command permissions only. Don't attempt to edit files or use other restricted tools.

guideline.name: Maintain Consistency
guideline.description: Use consistent terminology, command structure, and communication style throughout all interactions.

success_criteria.criterion: No errors or warnings in execution
For Java: both scans completed when needed
